heroOverview: Esophoria is a latent tendency of the eyes to deviate inward that is kept in check by the binocular fusion reflex during normal viewing; it affects an estimated 3-6% of the general population and is one of the most common forms of heterophoria encountered in clinical optometric and ophthalmological practice. Unlike esotropia — a constant or intermittent manifest inward eye turn — esophoria is only revealed by dissociation tests that interrupt fusion, such as covering one eye. Most people with esophoria are asymptomatic, but when the deviation exceeds the individual's fusional vergence reserve, it causes symptoms of visual fatigue, frontal headache, double vision, and difficulty sustaining near work such as reading or screen use. Esophoria at near fixation is most commonly driven by accommodative-convergence coupling: the act of focusing on a near object drives an inward vergence reflex that may exceed the diplopia threshold in susceptible individuals.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 3-6% of the general population; esophoria at near fixation is among the most common binocular vision diagnoses in optometry practice
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; near esophoria is common in school-age children and young adults performing intensive near work; distance esophoria is more often associated with hypermetropia
keyStats.avgAge: Can present at any age; symptomatic near esophoria is most frequently diagnosed in children aged 8-16 and working-age adults with high near-work demands
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence data vary by measurement method; approximately 1 in 20 individuals have measurable esophoria on cover testing

definition: Esophoria (ICD-10: H50.51) is a latent convergent deviation of the visual axes, classified within the heterophoria spectrum (latent strabismus). The condition is characterised by an inward tendency of one or both eyes that is suppressed by the active binocular fusion mechanism during normal binocular viewing. It becomes apparent only when fusion is interrupted, for example by covering one eye, by prism dissociation testing, or by extreme fatigue that reduces fusional vergence reserve. Esophoria is distinguished from esotropia by its latency — it produces no manifest eye turn under binocular conditions when the patient is alert. The magnitude is expressed in prism dioptres (PD) measured at distance (6 m) and at near (typically 40 cm). The condition falls within the heterophoria-heterotropia axis of strabismology, which is managed by ophthalmologists and optometrists specialising in binocular vision. Esophoria is considered pathological when the fusional divergence reserve is insufficient to maintain comfortable single binocular vision across all viewing demands; this state is termed decompensated esophoria and correlates with symptomatic asthenopia. Contributing factors include uncorrected or under-corrected hypermetropia (which drives excess accommodative convergence), convergence excess, reduced negative fusional vergence, fatigue, illness, and certain medications. Esophoria with a high accommodative convergence-to-accommodation ratio (AC/A ratio) is the most clinically common sub-pattern and responds most predictably to optical correction of hypermetropia.

typesClassification.description: Similar magnitude at distance and near; not primarily driven by the accommodative system and may indicate a tonic convergence excess or medial rectus overaction.
Greater deviation at near than at distance, driven by excessive accommodative convergence with a high AC/A ratio; the most common pattern, strongly associated with uncorrected hypermetropia.
Greater deviation at distance than at near; an uncommon pattern that in adults may indicate intracranial pathology (elevated ICP, dorsal midbrain lesion) and warrants neurological evaluation.
Esophoria in which the fusional divergence reserve is overwhelmed, producing symptomatic asthenopia, diplopia, or intermittent manifest esotropia; the primary target for clinical intervention.

primarySymptoms: Asthenopia — eye strain, heaviness, or aching of the eyes during near work such as reading, writing, or screen use, typically developing after 15-30 minutes of sustained near focus.
Frontal or brow headaches that occur at or after extended near tasks and resolve with rest or eye closure, a classic symptom of decompensated esophoria or convergence excess.

Intermittent horizontal diplopia (double vision) at near, which may be the chief complaint in significant decompensated esophoria; the double image is side by side (uncrossed) and resolves when one eye is closed.

Difficulty sustaining near work — affected individuals find it hard to read for long periods without losing their place or having to re-read lines, reflecting the fatigue of maintaining binocular fusion.
Blurred vision after prolonged near work, caused by accommodative and vergence fluctuations as the fusion mechanism tires.
Avoidance of or reluctance to engage in sustained near tasks — children may appear inattentive or dislike reading, which can be mistakenly attributed to learning difficulties.
A pulling, straining, or dragging sensation behind the eyes with near work, reflecting excessive convergence effort.
Photophobia and sensitivity to bright light or screen glare during or after extended near tasks.
Occasional blinking or rubbing of the eyes during near work as the patient attempts to refresh convergence and accommodation.
Symptoms worsening with fatigue, illness, or during periods of increased near-work demands such as examinations or intensive computer use.
earlyWarningSigns: A child who holds reading material unusually close, squints one eye when reading, or loses their place frequently on the page.
Reports of transient double vision that resolves quickly — the child may describe letters jumping or running together.
Unexplained headaches localised to the forehead that occur predictably after school or study sessions.
A parent noticing one eye appearing to briefly turn inward when the child is tired or unwell.
emergencySigns: New-onset constant diplopia at distance in an adult with no prior history of binocular vision problems — divergence insufficiency esophoria in adults can be caused by raised intracranial pressure, a dorsal midbrain lesion, or a sixth nerve palsy and requires urgent neurological assessment.
Sudden onset of large-angle manifest esotropia in an adult, particularly with associated headache or other neurological symptoms — this is a potential neurological emergency.
Esophoria identified after head injury or in the context of papilloedema — raised ICP must be excluded urgently.

causes.description: In hypermetropia (long-sightedness), the emmetropic focus point falls behind the retina; to achieve clear vision the ciliary muscle contracts to increase lens curvature (accommodation), and the high AC/A coupling means this accommodation drives a proportional inward convergence. If the convergence driven by accommodation exceeds the fusional divergence reserve, esophoria results.

In individuals with a high AC/A ratio, each dioptre of accommodation produces an above-normal convergence response; even a small amount of accommodative effort for near work generates a disproportionate inward vergence pull, commonly producing near esophoria in the absence of significant hypermetropia.

The fusional vergence system normally compensates for underlying phoric deviations; when the divergence amplitudes are subnormal — due to developmental, neurological, or fatigue-related causes — the esophoria decompensates and becomes symptomatic even at moderate magnitudes.

Structural variation in the orbital anatomy, medial rectus muscle tension, or the innervational balance of the oculomotor nuclei can produce a tonic convergent bias that underlies basic or distance esophoria independent of the accommodative system.

Divergence insufficiency — the pattern of greater esophoria at distance than at near — can result from elevated intracranial pressure, posterior fossa lesions, dorsal midbrain syndrome, or bilateral sixth nerve paresis; this pattern should prompt MRI and neurological evaluation, particularly in adults with no childhood history of strabismus.

causes.cause: Decompensation from fatigue, illness, or life demands
causes.description: Individuals with a pre-existing controlled esophoria can decompensate transiently during febrile illness, extreme fatigue, prolonged near work, or stress; in these periods, fusional reserves fall below the level needed to compensate the underlying deviation, producing episodic symptoms.

riskFactors.description: Even modest uncorrected long-sightedness of +1.00 to +2.00 dioptres can drive sufficient accommodative convergence to cause symptomatic near esophoria; full hypermetropic correction often eliminates or greatly reduces the deviation and associated symptoms.

Sustained near work increases accommodation and convergence load; individuals with borderline fusional reserves decompensate more readily under high near-work demands — a major factor driving presentation in school-age children and screen-based workers.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of strabismus or heterophoria
riskFactors.description: Binocular vision disorders including esophoria show familial clustering, though the precise genetic architecture is not fully established; a family history of squint increases the index of suspicion for binocular vision anomalies.
Infants born prematurely have higher rates of all forms of strabismus including esophoria-related binocular vision problems, likely due to disrupted oculomotor development during the critical period.
Traumatic brain injury, multiple sclerosis, and other neurological conditions alter brainstem vergence tone and fusional amplitude, increasing the risk of decompensated esophoria.

riskFactors.factor: Poor sleep, fatigue, and general illness
riskFactors.description: Fatigue reliably reduces fusional vergence reserves; individuals with controlled esophoria frequently report symptom onset or worsening during illness or sleep-deprived periods, which resolves with recovery.

affectedDemographics: School-age children with uncorrected hypermetropia who develop symptoms during increasing reading demands
Young adults and university students with heavy reading, studying, or screen-based workloads
Adults in occupations requiring sustained near work, including data entry, watchmaking, and surgery
Individuals with uncorrected or inadequately corrected refractive error
Patients with a history of strabismus surgery who develop post-operative esophoria as a complication
Adults with neurological conditions affecting vergence control, including traumatic brain injury
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of esophoria requires a structured binocular vision assessment that goes beyond standard visual acuity testing. The cornerstone is the cover test performed at distance (6 m) and near (40 cm): the alternating cover test reveals any latent deviation by alternately covering each eye and observing the uncovered eye for a refixation movement; an inward refixation movement indicates esophoria. The magnitude is quantified with prism bars or a prism cover test. The Maddox rod test dissociates the eyes using a red rod-and-point-light combination and allows subjective quantification of the phoria magnitude. Near point of convergence (NPC) measurement assesses whether the eyes can converge to the typical near point (approximately 8-10 cm); a receded NPC (beyond 10 cm) indicates convergence insufficiency rather than excess, an important differential. Fusional vergence amplitudes are measured using prism bars in the divergence (base-in) direction: reduced negative fusional vergence is the functional bottleneck in decompensated esophoria. The AC/A ratio is calculated as the difference in deviation between distance and near divided by the accommodative demand, or using the gradient method with accommodative lenses; a ratio above 6:1 (PD per dioptre) is considered elevated. Cycloplegic refraction is essential, particularly in children, to reveal any latent hypermetropia masked by active accommodation. Fixation disparity testing with Mallett units or Wesson cards identifies the small residual binocular stress under real viewing conditions and guides prism prescription. In adults with divergence insufficiency esophoria (greater at distance than at near), neuroimaging and neurological assessment are mandatory to exclude intracranial pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and characterises the latent deviation; the alternating cover test interrupts fusion and reveals the full magnitude of esophoria as an inward refixation movement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner covers each eye in turn with a paddle while you fixate a target; the procedure is quick, painless, and takes about two minutes at each fixation distance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the exact magnitude of the esophoria in prism dioptres at distance and near by neutralising the refixation movement with a prism placed before the eye.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner holds prisms of increasing strength in front of your eye while repeatedly covering and uncovering it; it is entirely painless and takes three to five minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the range of base-in (divergence) prism the eyes can sustain while maintaining single binocular vision; a reduced divergence amplitude at near indicates a decompensated esophoria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You look at a target while prisms are gradually increased until you see double; you then report when the image becomes single again as the prism is reduced. The test is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Subjectively quantifies the heterophoria by dissociating the two eyes with a red rod filter; the patient sees a red line (one eye) and a white light (the other) and describes their relative position.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A red-striated lens is placed before one eye in a trial frame; you describe where the red line appears relative to a distant white light. Takes two to three minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reveals the full degree of hypermetropia including the latent component that is masked by active accommodation; essential for planning optical treatment of accommodative esophoria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Cyclopentolate eye drops are instilled; your near vision blurs completely for four to six hours while the drops work, and refraction is performed 30-40 minutes after instillation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines the amount of convergence driven per unit of accommodation; a high AC/A confirms accommodative convergence excess as the mechanism, guiding bifocal or progressive lens prescription.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The cover test magnitude is measured with and without a +3.00 lens before each eye; the difference divided by 3 gives the AC/A ratio. No drops or physical contact required.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of esophoria is indicated when the deviation is decompensated and causes symptoms that impair reading, near work, or quality of life, or when there is a risk of manifest esotropia developing. The first and most important step is full correction of the refractive error: prescribing the full cycloplegic hypermetropic refraction in children with accommodative esophoria often normalises or dramatically reduces the deviation. Where the near esophoria persists despite full refractive correction, bifocal or progressive addition lenses reduce the accommodation required at near, thereby reducing the accommodative convergence drive and improving comfort. Vision therapy — a structured programme of vergence and accommodative exercises performed in a clinic and at home — has the strongest evidence base for convergence excess esophoria with reduced fusional reserves; it expands divergence amplitude, improves fusional flexibility, and reduces symptoms in the majority of motivated patients. Base-in prisms reduce the demand on divergence by shifting the optical axis; they are prescribed when vision therapy is ineffective or not feasible and can be incorporated into spectacle lenses. Botulinum toxin injection and surgical correction of the medial rectus (recession) are reserved for large, stable, symptomatic deviations that have not responded to optical and vision therapy measures and are rarely required. For divergence insufficiency esophoria in adults, the priority is neurological workup to exclude intracranial pathology; if the deviation is benign, small base-in prisms are the preferred management as surgery carries risks in this setting.

medicalTreatments.name: Full hypermetropic spectacle or contact lens correction
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribing the full cycloplegic refraction eliminates the accommodative drive to convergence in hypermetropic patients; in high-AC/A esophoria this alone may reduce or normalise the deviation. Worn consistently as the primary optical correction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces or eliminates the near deviation in approximately 70-80% of children with accommodative convergence excess when the full hypermetropic correction is worn consistently.

medicalTreatments.name: Bifocal or progressive addition spectacle lenses
medicalTreatments.description: Adding a positive reading addition (typically +1.50 to +3.00 D) to the near segment reduces the accommodation required for close work, thereby lowering the convergence drive; used when full distance correction alone does not control the near deviation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls symptomatic near esophoria in approximately 60-75% of children and adults with high AC/A ratio when the addition is correctly calculated and consistently worn.

medicalTreatments.name: Base-in prism spectacle lenses
medicalTreatments.description: Fresnel or ground-in base-in prisms incorporated into spectacles reduce the demand on negative fusional vergence by shifting the position of the image toward the fovea; prescribed after vergence therapy when fusional expansion is insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptomatic asthenopia in approximately 55-70% of patients with decompensated esophoria; most effective when the prism dose matches the fixation disparity rather than the full phoria magnitude.

medicalTreatments.name: Vision therapy (orthoptic exercises)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured programme of vergence exercises — including pencil push-ups, Brock string, aperture rule, and computer-based vergence training — performed under clinician supervision and at home; designed to expand divergence amplitude and improve fusional flexibility and sustenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised controlled trials show clinically significant improvement in symptoms and vergence measures in approximately 70-80% of motivated patients with convergence excess esophoria completing a 12-week programme.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection to medial rectus
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of botulinum toxin type A into one or both medial rectus muscles temporarily reduces their contractile force, reducing the convergent bias; used as a non-surgical alternative in selected adults with symptomatic esophoria refractory to optical and vergence therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces temporary reduction of esophoria in approximately 50-60% of treated adults; effects typically last 3-6 months and may need to be repeated.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral medial rectus recession
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical weakening of both medial rectus muscles by detaching and reattaching them more posteriorly on the globe; reserved for large (typically >25 PD), stable, symptomatic esophorias or decompensated intermittent esotropias that have failed non-surgical management. Performed under general anaesthesia as a day procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves alignment within 10 PD of orthophoria in approximately 70-80% of cases at one year; long-term success rates are somewhat lower due to the natural tendency for convergent deviation to persist.

alternativeTreatments.name: Home-based vergence exercises (pencil push-ups, Brock string)
alternativeTreatments.description: Simple free-space vergence exercises can be performed at home daily; while not as effective as office-based vision therapy in randomised trials, they are a low-cost adjunct and are recommended as home practice between clinic sessions in structured vision therapy programmes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Computer-based vergence training applications
alternativeTreatments.description: Software programmes (e.g. HTS, home vision therapy systems) deliver calibrated vergence and accommodative exercises via a standard monitor; they supplement clinic-based orthoptics and improve compliance by making exercises accessible and measurable at home.

whySeeSpecialist: An ophthalmologist or optometrist specialising in binocular vision and strabismus should be seen when esophoria causes symptoms sufficient to impair reading, near work, or quality of life; when a child shows avoidance of reading or unexplained academic difficulties; when the deviation is intermittently manifest (breaking down to esotropia); or when divergence insufficiency esophoria is identified in an adult without a prior history of strabismus, which requires exclusion of neurological pathology by imaging. Vision therapy is most effective when supervised by a certified orthoptist or binocular vision specialist.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ophthalmologist with a strabismus and binocular vision subspecialty, or an optometrist with Fellowship or Diploma qualification in binocular vision (FBCLA, DCLP, or equivalent). In children, a paediatric ophthalmologist with an orthoptist should assess the full refractive and strabismus picture. Ask whether the practice offers supervised orthoptic exercises, fixation disparity testing with Mallett units, and full cycloplegic refraction, as these are the key components of a thorough evaluation. For vision therapy, a clinic affiliated with COVD (College of Optometrists in Vision Development) in the US, or with the British and Irish Orthoptic Society in the UK, provides evidence-based structured programmes.
preventionStrategies: Have refractive errors corrected fully with up-to-date spectacle prescriptions — this is the single most effective modifiable factor for preventing symptomatic accommodative esophoria.
In children with a family history of strabismus, arrange regular vision screening from the preschool years to detect hypermetropia and binocular vision anomalies before they become symptomatic.
Apply the 20-20-20 rule for screen-based near work — every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet (6 m) away for 20 seconds — to reduce the continuous convergence and accommodative load.
Ensure adequate lighting for reading and near work to reduce the accommodative effort required.
Prioritise sleep and manage fatigue, which reliably reduces fusional vergence reserves and can cause symptomatic decompensation.
For patients with known esophoria, increase monitoring frequency during periods of high near-work demand such as school examinations or new job demands.
lifestyleModifications: Follow the 20-20-20 rule during prolonged screen use or reading to give the vergence system regular breaks.
Ensure reading material is held at an appropriate working distance (approximately 35-40 cm) rather than very close, which increases convergence demand.
Optimise computer screen distance (approximately 50-70 cm) and height (centre of screen at or slightly below eye level) to reduce neck strain and excessive downward gaze convergence.
Use good ambient lighting for near work; avoid reading in dim light, which increases strain on the accommodative and vergence systems.
When symptoms are worse during fatigue or illness, temporarily reduce near-work demands and increase rest breaks.
Keep spectacle lens prescriptions up to date — even small changes in refractive error significantly affect the accommodative convergence balance.

recommended: Antioxidant-rich diet with leafy greens, berries, and nuts to support general neurological and ocular health
Adequate hydration — mild dehydration is associated with reduced concentration and increased visual fatigue that can exacerbate vergence difficulties
Magnesium-rich foods (leafy greens, nuts, whole grains) which support neuromuscular function including oculomotor control
avoid: Excessive caffeine, which can exacerbate eye strain and disrupt sleep, thereby reducing fusional reserves
Heavy alcohol consumption, which impairs brainstem vergence control and can transiently worsen esophoria

exerciseGuidelines: General physical activity and aerobic exercise support healthy brainstem and oculomotor function and do not worsen esophoria. Contact sports with a risk of head injury should be noted, as traumatic brain injury is a recognised cause of vergence disorders including decompensated esophoria. Home vergence exercises prescribed as part of a vision therapy programme should be performed consistently (typically 15-20 minutes daily), with the frequency and difficulty adjusted under clinician guidance to progressively challenge the divergence system without causing excessive fatigue.
dailyManagement: Wear prescribed spectacles or contact lenses consistently, particularly for near work — removing correction increases accommodative demand and worsens convergence.
Perform prescribed home vergence exercises (pencil push-ups, Brock string, or software-based programme) daily as directed by the orthoptist or vision therapist.
Apply the 20-20-20 break rule at screen, book, or desk work.
Log symptoms in a diary (severity, duration, time of day, activity) to track progress and help the clinician adjust the management plan.
Report any new or worsening diplopia at distance to the clinician promptly, as divergence insufficiency esophoria in adults requires neurological investigation.
Attend follow-up appointments at scheduled intervals to monitor fusional amplitude progress and adjust prism or spectacle prescriptions as needed.
prognosis: The prognosis for esophoria is generally good when the deviation is managed appropriately. Accommodative esophoria associated with hypermetropia in children often improves over time as accommodation normalises toward emmetropia; many children no longer require treatment or full hypermetropic correction by their mid-teens. Convergence excess esophoria treated with vision therapy achieves symptomatic resolution in 70-80% of compliant patients. Decompensated esophoria managed with base-in prisms provides reliable symptom control, though the deviation itself persists. Adults with divergence insufficiency esophoria from a benign cause have a stable course when managed with small prisms; those with an underlying neurological cause are dependent on the trajectory of that condition. Surgery for large esophorias achieves initial alignment in the majority but has a recurrence risk of approximately 20-30% over five years, reflecting the persistent binocular drive toward convergence.
recoveryTimeline: Children undergoing full hypermetropic correction for accommodative esophoria see the greatest improvement in the first four to eight weeks as the accommodative-convergence relationship stabilises with consistent spectacle wear; the deviation may reduce further over three to six months. Vision therapy programmes show the most significant vergence amplitude gains at 8-12 weeks, with continued improvement over 16-24 weeks for full consolidation. Prism prescriptions provide immediate symptomatic relief within days of dispensing. After strabismus surgery, eye alignment is assessed at six to eight weeks and the final outcome is typically stable by three months.
complications: Manifest esotropia — prolonged or severe decompensated esophoria can break down to a constant or intermittent manifest inward eye turn, particularly in children; this requires prompt treatment to prevent amblyopia.

Amblyopia — if esophoria breaks down to manifest esotropia, the non-fixating eye may develop reduced visual acuity due to cortical suppression; early detection and patching or optical treatment is essential.
Diplopia — persistent horizontal double vision significantly impairs driving, reading, and occupational function, and may require prism glasses or surgery to resolve.

Reduced reading ability and academic performance — undetected esophoria in children causes reading-related asthenopia that may be misattributed to attention deficit disorder or dyslexia; formal binocular vision assessment should be part of educational vision screening.
Psychological impact — persistent headaches and visual discomfort from decompensated esophoria affect quality of life and can cause significant anxiety if the cause is not identified and explained.

supportResources.name: College of Optometrists — Binocular vision and strabismus
supportResources.description: UK clinical guidance on the diagnosis and management of heterophoria and strabismus, including esophoria.

supportResources.name: RNIB — Visual stress and binocular vision problems
supportResources.description: UK resource with information on binocular vision problems, visual fatigue, and where to find specialist assessment.

supportResources.name: American Association for Pediatric Ophthalmology and Strabismus (AAPOS) — Esophoria
supportResources.description: Patient information on esophoria diagnosis, treatment options including surgery, and the difference between esophoria and esotropia.

sources.title: Scheiman M, Wick B. Clinical Management of Binocular Vision: Heterophoric, Accommodative, and Eye Movement Disorders (4th ed). Lippincott Williams & Wilkins, 2014
CITT Study Group. Treatment of Symptomatic Convergence Insufficiency in Children. JAMA Ophthalmology, 2019
Vidal-Galianes A et al. Prevalence of binocular vision disorders in schoolchildren. Graefe's Archive for Clinical and Experimental Ophthalmology, 2011
Wick B. Accommodative esotropia: efficacy of therapy. Journal of the American Optometric Association, 1987
Horwood AM, Riddell PM. Heterophoria control strategies in free viewing. Ophthalmic and Physiological Optics, 2013
NHS Clinical Knowledge Summary — Strabismus in children
American Academy of Ophthalmology — Esophoria and Esotropia
Evans BJW. Pickwell's Binocular Vision Anomalies (5th ed). Butterworth-Heinemann, 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Convergence insufficiency causes a divergent phoria at near (exophoria) with a receded near point of convergence, producing symptoms during near work that are almost identical to esophoria but require opposite treatment — convergence training rather than divergence training.

confusedWithConditions.name: Exophoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Exophoria is a latent outward deviation — the opposite direction to esophoria. Both cause asthenopia with near work but diverge in cover test direction, prism direction needed, and underlying refractive associations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Monocular esotropia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Esotropia is a manifest (constantly visible) inward eye turn present under binocular viewing conditions, whereas esophoria is latent and only revealed by covering one eye or dissociation testing.

relatedConditions.name: Exophoria
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite latent deviation; both are heterophorias managed by vergence rehabilitation, but exophoria requires convergence training while esophoria requires divergence training.

relatedConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Convergence insufficiency and convergence excess esophoria both cause near-work asthenopia; distinguishing them by cover test direction and NPC measurement is critical to selecting the correct treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Convergence excess
relatedConditions.relevance: Convergence excess describes the mechanism underlying the most common form of esophoria; addressing it with full hypermetropic correction and vision therapy is the primary treatment pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Diplopia
relatedConditions.relevance: Decompensated esophoria is a leading cause of acquired horizontal diplopia at near; managing the underlying phoria resolves the diplopia in the majority of cases.

faqs.question: What is esophoria?
faqs.answer: Esophoria is a latent tendency for the eyes to turn inward. It is kept under control by the brain's fusion reflex during normal vision, but becomes apparent when one eye is covered. When fusion cannot compensate, it causes eye strain, headaches, and double vision.

faqs.question: Is esophoria the same as a squint?
faqs.answer: No. Esophoria is a latent (hidden) inward deviation revealed only by covering one eye. A squint (esotropia) is a manifest inward turn visible during normal binocular viewing. Esophoria can break down to esotropia if untreated, but they are distinct conditions requiring different management.

faqs.question: What causes esophoria?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is uncorrected long-sightedness (hypermetropia), which drives excessive convergence when focusing on near objects. A naturally high convergence response, reduced ability to diverge, fatigue, and neurological conditions can also contribute.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of esophoria?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include eye strain, aching eyes, frontal headaches, and difficulty sustaining reading or screen work. Intermittent double vision at near, blurred vision after near tasks, and an aversion to reading are also common, particularly in children.

faqs.question: How is esophoria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: An optometrist or ophthalmologist performs a cover test to detect the latent deviation, prism testing to measure its size, and fusional vergence amplitude testing to assess compensation. Cycloplegic refraction reveals any hidden long-sightedness. The AC/A ratio may be calculated to guide treatment.

faqs.question: Can esophoria be cured?
faqs.answer: Accommodative esophoria in children often improves as refractive error normalises with age. Vision therapy achieves sustained symptom resolution in 70-80% of patients with convergence excess. With appropriate glasses, exercises, or prisms, most people achieve comfortable single binocular vision.

faqs.question: What is vision therapy for esophoria?
faqs.answer: Vision therapy is a structured programme of exercises designed to expand the eyes' divergence range. It includes Brock string exercises, aperture rule, and computer-based vergence training performed in clinic and at home, typically over 12-24 weeks under the supervision of an orthoptist.

faqs.question: Do glasses help esophoria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Full correction of long-sightedness reduces the accommodative convergence driving the deviation. Bifocal or progressive addition lenses further reduce near convergence demand in patients with a high AC/A ratio. Base-in prism lenses can be added when optical correction alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: Can children have esophoria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Esophoria is common in school-age children, particularly those with uncorrected long-sightedness. It can cause reading difficulty, headaches, and avoidance of near work that is sometimes mistaken for attention or learning problems. A full binocular vision assessment by an optometrist is important.

faqs.question: Is esophoria serious?
faqs.answer: Mild, compensated esophoria causing no symptoms requires no treatment. Decompensated esophoria interfering with near work needs management to prevent worsening and avoid breakdown to manifest esotropia or amblyopia in children. New esophoria at distance in adults may occasionally indicate neurological disease.

faqs.question: What is decompensated esophoria?
faqs.answer: Decompensated esophoria is when the fusional divergence reserve is no longer sufficient to prevent symptoms or to maintain single binocular vision. It is the form that requires treatment, and is characterised by eye strain, headaches, and intermittent double vision with near work.

faqs.question: Can esophoria cause amblyopia?
faqs.answer: If esophoria breaks down to a manifest esotropia, the brain may suppress the image from the deviating eye, leading to amblyopia (lazy eye) particularly in young children under seven. This is why untreated decompensated esophoria in children needs prompt assessment.

faqs.question: What exercises help esophoria?
faqs.answer: Divergence exercises are the primary approach — including pencil push-aways (moving a pencil away from the face while keeping it single), Brock string at distance, and base-in prism reading exercises. These are most effective when prescribed and supervised by a binocular vision specialist.

faqs.question: Does screen time worsen esophoria?
faqs.answer: Prolonged screen use increases the continuous convergence demand, which can fatigue the fusional system in susceptible individuals. Following the 20-20-20 rule — 20-second breaks looking at distance every 20 minutes — and maintaining correct screen distance reduce this load.

faqs.question: Can esophoria cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Frontal or brow headaches occurring after reading or screen work are one of the most common presentations of decompensated esophoria. They reflect the effort of maintaining fusion against the inward deviation. Correcting the underlying refractive error and improving divergence reserves typically resolves the headaches.

faqs.question: How is esophoria different from exophoria?
faqs.answer: Esophoria is a latent inward deviation; exophoria is a latent outward deviation. Esophoria is associated with long-sightedness and a high convergence drive; exophoria is often associated with short-sightedness or reduced convergence. Both cause eye strain, but their treatment directions are opposite.

faqs.question: When does esophoria need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rarely needed for esophoria alone. It is considered for large, stable esophorias greater than approximately 25 prism dioptres that have failed optical correction and vision therapy, or where the esophoria has broken down to a constant esotropia. Bilateral medial rectus recession is the standard procedure.

faqs.question: Can an adult develop esophoria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults can develop esophoria from neurological causes (raised intracranial pressure, dorsal midbrain lesion), after head injury, or following a change in refractive error. New-onset esophoria greater at distance than near in an adult without childhood strabismus requires neurological evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the AC/A ratio and why does it matter in esophoria?
faqs.answer: The AC/A ratio measures how much convergence is generated per dioptre of accommodation. A high ratio means focusing on near objects drives a disproportionate inward pull, causing near esophoria. Measuring it guides whether bifocal lenses or vision therapy are the most appropriate management.

faqs.question: Does esophoria affect driving?
faqs.answer: Well-compensated esophoria does not impair driving. If esophoria causes intermittent diplopia or significant near-work disability, this should be disclosed to licensing authorities as applicable. Driving at night or in rain can fatigue the fusional system more quickly in individuals with borderline compensation.

metaDescription: Esophoria is a latent inward eye deviation causing headaches, eye strain, and double vision. Learn causes, diagnosis, and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
